request.question: Kyle MacLachlan's role as Dale Cooper was featured on what network in 2017?

sentences: Twin Peaks, also known as Twin Peaks: The Return, is an American mystery drama television revival series created by Mark Frost and David Lynch.
It is a continuation of the 1990–91 ABC series of the same name.
The limited series consists of 18 episodes and premiered on Showtime on May 21, 2017, following a world premiere on May 19, 2017, at The Theatre at Ace Hotel.
The series was developed and written by Lynch and Frost over several years and directed by Lynch.
An ensemble of returning and new cast members appear, led by original star Kyle MacLachlan.

FBI Special Agent Dale Bartholomew Cooper, portrayed by Kyle MacLachlan, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the ABC television series "Twin Peaks" and Showtime's continuation of the same name.
He also plays a supporting role in the prequel film "".
Audrey Horne is a fictional character from the ABC television series "Twin Peaks", played by Sherilyn Fenn.
The character was created by David Lynch.
She was introduced in the pilot.

The daughter of Ben (Richard Beymer) and Sylvia Horne, sister of Johnny Horne (Robert Bauer) and half-sister of Donna Hayward (Lara Flynn Boyle), her storylines focused on her infatuation with the series protagonist Dale Cooper (Kyle MacLachlan), infiltrating the brothel/casino One Eyed Jacks and becoming an activist through civil disobedience.
Orson Hodge is a fictional character on the ABC television series "Desperate Housewives".
The character is played by Kyle MacLachlan.
Orson is introduced in the final episodes of the second season of the series, and becomes the main mystery of the third season.
MacLachlan left the main cast in season six, but made guest appearances until the eighth and final season.
"Episode 4", also known as "The One-Armed Man", is the fifth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".
The episode was written by Robert Engels, and directed by Tim Hunter.
"Episode 4" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Piper Laurie and Richard Beymer, and introduces series co-creator David Lynch in the role of Gordon Cole.
"Episode 5", also known as "Cooper's Dreams", is the sixth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".
The episode was written by series co-creator Mark Frost and directed by Lesli Linka Glatter.
"Episode 5" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Richard Beymer, with guest appearances by Chris Mulkey and David Patrick Kelly.
"Blue Velvet" is a 1986 American mystery film written and directed by David Lynch.
The movie exhibits elements of both film noir and surrealism.
The film features Kyle MacLachlan, Isabella Rossellini, Dennis Hopper, and Laura Dern.
The title is taken from the 1963 Bobby Vinton song of the same name, which is featured in the film.
Although initially detested by some mainstream critics, the film has now become widely acclaimed.
Kyle Merritt MacLachlan ( ; born February 22, 1959) is an American actor.

MacLachlan is best known for his role as Dale Cooper in "Twin Peaks" (1990–1991; 2017) and its film prequel "" (1992), as well as roles in two of David Lynch's films: Paul Atreides in "Dune" (1984) and Jeffrey Beaumont in "Blue Velvet" (1986).

MacLachlan's other film roles include Lloyd Gallagher in "The Hidden" (1987), Ray Manzarek in "The Doors" (1991), Clifford Vandercave in "The Flintstones" (1994), Zack Carey in "Showgirls" (1995), and the voice of Riley's dad in "Inside Out" (2015).
Valerie "Val" Cooper is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character works for the Office of National Emergency.
She is most notable for her work as the liaison for mutant affairs in the United States government.

She once claimed to have been inspired to government service by the interesting cases her brother, an FBI agent, encountered in his work (it was implied, but never stated outright, that her brother is Dale Cooper from "Twin Peaks").
The pilot episode, also known as Northwest Passage, of the mystery television series "Twin Peaks" premiered on the ABC Network on Sunday, April 8, 1990.
It was written by series creators Mark Frost and David Lynch, and directed by Lynch.

The pilot follows the characters of Dale Cooper and Harry S. Truman as they investigate the death of popular high school student Laura Palmer; Cooper believes the murder has connections to a murder case that occurred a year earlier.
In addition to setting the tone for the show, the episode sets up several character and story arcs and marked the appearance of several recurring characters.
The episode received a strong Nielsen household rating compared to other season one episodes and was well received by fans and critics alike.
The original title for the series was "Northwest Passage", but this was later changed.

candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

hop1_docs: Kyle MacLachlan | Kyle Merritt MacLachlan ( ; born February 22, 1959) is an American actor.  MacLachlan is best known for his role as Dale Cooper in "Twin Peaks" (1990–1991; 2017) and its film prequel "" (1992), as well as roles in two of David Lynch's films: Paul Atreides in "Dune" (1984) and Jeffrey Beaumont in "Blue Velvet" (1986).  MacLachlan's other film roles include Lloyd Gallagher in "The Hidden" (1987), Ray Manzarek in "The Doors" (1991), Clifford Vandercave in "The Flintstones" (1994), Zack Carey in "Showgirls" (1995), and the voice of Riley's dad in "Inside Out" (2015).

Dale Cooper | FBI Special Agent Dale Bartholomew Cooper, portrayed by Kyle MacLachlan, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the ABC television series "Twin Peaks" and Showtime's continuation of the same name.  He also plays a supporting role in the prequel film "".

Audrey Horne | Audrey Horne is a fictional character from the ABC television series "Twin Peaks", played by Sherilyn Fenn.  The character was created by David Lynch.  She was introduced in the pilot.  The daughter of Ben (Richard Beymer) and Sylvia Horne, sister of Johnny Horne (Robert Bauer) and half-sister of Donna Hayward (Lara Flynn Boyle), her storylines focused on her infatuation with the series protagonist Dale Cooper (Kyle MacLachlan), infiltrating the brothel/casino One Eyed Jacks and becoming an activist through civil disobedience.

Episode 5 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 5", also known as "Cooper's Dreams", is the sixth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by series co-creator Mark Frost and directed by Lesli Linka Glatter.  "Episode 5" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Richard Beymer, with guest appearances by Chris Mulkey and David Patrick Kelly.

Episode 3 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 3", later also known as "Rest in Pain", is the fourth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by Harley Peyton, and directed by Tina Rathborne.  "Episode 3" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Ray Wise, and introduces Sheryl Lee's second role, Maddy Ferguson.

Episode 4 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 4", also known as "The One-Armed Man", is the fifth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by Robert Engels, and directed by Tim Hunter.  "Episode 4" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Piper Laurie and Richard Beymer, and introduces series co-creator David Lynch in the role of Gordon Cole.

The Hidden (film) | The Hidden is an American science fiction action-horror film produced and released in 1987 by New Line Cinema.  The film was written by Bob Hunt (pen name of writer/producer/director Jim Kouf) and directed by Jack Sholder.  The cast features Kyle MacLachlan and Michael Nouri and includes supporting roles by Clu Gulager, Chris Mulkey, Ed O'Ross, Clarence Felder, Claudia Christian and Larry Cedar.  A sequel, "The Hidden II", directed by Seth Pinsker was released in 1993.
response.hop2_query: "Kyle MacLachlan Dale Cooper 2017 network" "Twin Peaks: The Return 2017 aired on Showtime" site:news OR site:variety OR site:hollywoodreporter OR site:imdb.com OR site:showtime.com
hop2_docs: Dale Cooper | FBI Special Agent Dale Bartholomew Cooper, portrayed by Kyle MacLachlan, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the ABC television series "Twin Peaks" and Showtime's continuation of the same name.  He also plays a supporting role in the prequel film "".

Twin Peaks (2017 TV series) | Twin Peaks, also known as Twin Peaks: The Return, is an American mystery drama television revival series created by Mark Frost and David Lynch.  It is a continuation of the 1990–91 ABC series of the same name.  The limited series consists of 18 episodes and premiered on Showtime on May 21, 2017, following a world premiere on May 19, 2017, at The Theatre at Ace Hotel.  The series was developed and written by Lynch and Frost over several years and directed by Lynch.  An ensemble of returning and new cast members appear, led by original star Kyle MacLachlan.

TheSabre | TheSabre.com is an independent online publication covering University of Virginia Cavaliers athletics.  The site, originally named "VirginiaFootball.com", was established by Charlottesville, Virginia resident and former U.S. Air Force Security Specialist Michael Ingalls the fall of 1996.  The site changed its name to "TheSabre.com" in 1998 because the site moved into more than just football and basketball coverage.  The change also facilitated greater cooperation by the University of Virginia athletic department as there was some concern over trademark issues - the site held two domain names at the time; previously mentioned "VirginiaFootball.com" as well as "VirginiaBasketball.com".

Gay.com | Gay.com is a chat, personals, and social networking website catering to the LGBT community.  The site is a digital brand of Here Media Inc. In addition to community features, the site features LGBT-related news and features.  As of September 2005, "San Jose Mercury News" ranked gay.com as the most popular online gay personals site in the United States.  As of March 2007, Hitwise ranked it number three in domestic American popularity after Adam4Adam and Manhunt.  Gay.com also competes internationally with dudesnude, gayromeo, and gaydar.

Heavy.com | Heavy.com is a news and information website based in New York City.  Founded in 1999 as an entertainment website by Simon Assaad and David Carson, the site switched to news and popular information content in 2012.  The site is owned by Heavy Inc., which also owns the Spanish-language news site Ahoramismo.com.  Assaad is the CEO of Heavy Inc.

MiGente.com | Launched in 2000, the current iteration of MiGente.com is an online social-networking site specifically targeting the Hispanic community.  Its former parent company, Community Connect Inc., claimed that MiGente.com was the fastest growing English language site for the Hispanic community with over 3 million registered members.  Members come from a variety of Hispanic backgrounds.

ABCnews.com.co | ABCnews.com.co is a fake news site which mimics the URL, design and logo of the actual news site ABCnews.com (which is owned by the Disney–ABC Television Group).  Many stories from ABCnews.com.co have gone viral before being debunked.
